speaker: interviewer
text: Saul Stermer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my brother was wounded. And this man, this SS, he run away. And what happened after, we right away moved from Korolowka to [INAUDIBLE], where it was more safe. Why we moved there, a couple, a women like this Josel Dodek. He was there in the cave with his wife, [INAUDIBLE]. And he had a two-year-old son. She went to bring some milk for the in Korolowka. She never came back. Ukrainians kill her. This Josel was a very brave guy, very brave. He went to look all over for her. He even went to the cave. He thought maybe they brought her there. And one day, he went to this guy, what he, what he was shooting at my brother. And as he came in in the barn and he start looking, the guy was lying there and just, and killed Josel. This guy lived through the war and then he left a three-year-old child. Now, what happened with us was after the war was the biggest tragedy with our family yet. My father was a business man. And right away, he had everything. He had business, horses, [INAUDIBLE]. One day, about it was by the end of November, beginning of December, he went to [INAUDIBLE] to bring up some [INAUDIBLE]. And under on the way back came a gang of bandits, [NON-ENGLISH] as they are called. They took him out of the wagon and they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944 at the beginning of December, like that. Shall I say that I made the casket for my father? I made a casket for my father, myself. And my brother Nathan, Nissen, helped to dig the grave for my father, this was. But the Russians took my brother-in-law Fischel Dodek to the army. See they took him to the army and he was on [INAUDIBLE] I think or [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE]. Anyway, he was very capable. And he arranged a trip home with a few Ukrainians, plus him and a Russian, to go to our area because we had the, in Russia they were shortage-- by us, we had everything-- to bring some parcels for the other people, the workers. And we knew that he has to come home. And one day, this was the middle of the winter, might beginning of January maybe, something like that. Ukrainian guy and a Russian guy came into our house and says official with another village is coming. He had to, he didn't have transportation the same day with another Ukrainians. He remained there. At night they killed him. He left his wife and two small children. This was the tragedies here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Another tragedy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we lived in Borschiv.

speaker: interviewer
text: For how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed till we left. We left sometimes in end of August. Yeah, I think it was the end of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, we were there, well, you see, they want take me to the army. But I went to the authorities there, to the chief of the police. They had, the building was windows broke and this [INAUDIBLE] said, listen. I can't fix you all this here. He says, you know how to do things? I says, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, come in, come to work for us. I didn't have no tools, no nothing. I went to a guy. I bought some saw and a hammer this. And I came there and I was working there. So that I save myself to go to the army. Because a lot had went to the army and they never came. We had a cousin, he went to the army. He is survived with a wife and a child. And he got killed. So I went through the winter 19-- the summer 1944, the winter 1944, '45 working there. And in the spring, in May was end-- you know, how you call it-- the liberation, end of the war, and then we went all to Poland. We had the right to go because we were Polish citizens. So we had the right to go to Poland. We load up our stuff. And we came to Poland. We came to Bytom in 1945. And then in Bytom, they the Poles were not too nice. There was a program in Kielce, and here and there. And we said we have to go away from here, so me and my wife. When had you gotten married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in '46, 12th of April. already in the P camp where she was my [INAUDIBLE], my friend. Anyway, we went, me and my brother and his wife, we went and we came get to Vienna, from Vienna on the Americans zone. Then I came back for the rest of the family. The rest of the family, we came back to Germany. We all settled there. Now, we were in the P camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was Austria. My mother was in Germany. My sister was in Germany. So we wanted to go out from somewhere from there. We tried to go to the states. We couldn't. So once they came from the [? union. ?] I think it was a few, quite a few Jewish people. And they register.

speaker: interviewer
text: They say that, who is a tailor? To register himself and to go to the tailor. They need the tailors in Canada. So my brother says, look here, let's register. So we thought we'd registered and that's all, we registered. And one day, they called us, and he says, you have to come down to examine if you are a tailor. So we're coming down and we see, because a lot of people. Nobody had a-- nobody knew who I am. I come in there and I see that there's is they set up machines. And they call names and people go in. They put them the machine or they give them something to do. I said to my brother, listen, we are not tailors. We can't go in there, we might make themselves stupid. He says, you know something? We can send in somebody there for us. You want to go to Canada? What is that? We'll come there. We'll be tailors. So he says it's a good idea. But I have to tell a little story what it was. Once my brother was in the camp and a guy walked around, it was around in November or December, without a coat. My brother tells him, why don't you wear a coat? He says, I have none. I have no coat. I have nothing to wear.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, come into my house. He come to our house, he takes off an old coat that he came with Russia, a torn coat. He say, I have this coat. I can give you this coat, but look here. It's torn. He says, I'm a tailor. I'll fix it for myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I say, you remember this guy? He lives, he's in the other camp there. Let's find him. And as we walk to the bus stop, the guy is by the bus stop. And he is wearing my brother's coat. Say listen, come to our house. He comes. Tell him to start, look here. We are not tailors. We register so we want to go to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I'll do anything for you. I'll go in and I'll even. I said, but we need another tailor. He says my brother was also a tailor. Said bring him in. He says, we go away to Canada. You take all your stuff, everything is yours. We brought him down. We came in and we says Nissen Stermer is here and Saul Stermer is here. So they say, sit down there. They'll call you. They called my brother's name. We tell you, go in. I was sitting and watching the way the guy does the work, because there nobody knew who I am. I see they put him on machine scene. Perfect. The other guy, they put him on coat. I remember to put in a sleeve.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we came to Canada tailors. We came to Canada. We don't know. We register. We sign they have to be a year a tailor. I came into the factory. I'm not a tailor. But they put me to electric machine and they brought me over a bundle of gabardine belts. I thought, I believed in myself. I thought, I can do everything here. What is it? I can make this here too. I tried this electric machine and this belt, just gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I spoiled a few belt. The boss came over. He was a Jewish man, Mr. [INAUDIBLE]. He says to me, young man, you are not a tailor. He said listen, you are [INAUDIBLE] a tailor. He calls the foreman, Joe, put him to the press. He put me to the press. Right away, I was good. And in a few months, I was a top presser. And I made like three people. I made $85, $80 in 1948 with. The first year, I made $18. But then, and I was in Winnipeg. This was in Winnipeg. Then my brother was in Montreal. We came back here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was in the states. She was with my brother in the states. And we say listen, we have to do something ourself. So we went and bought a farm. Money we didn't [INAUDIBLE], a farm [INAUDIBLE]. We start keeping [INAUDIBLE] we see it, let's start doing some business. So we start buying junk. And this and sold it. We made some [INAUDIBLE]. Then we made a few dollars and said, listen, we are good in construction. Let's start construction. We find another guy with some money. And we bought some lots. And we were doing very good. Today, I am Canada, then I made a good deal in Canada, but Canada also didn't make a bad deal with me. Pay a lot of taxes and I keep a lot of people working, which is good for both of us. But I had to lie a little bit to come in here. They didn't let in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Stermer--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you mentioned before that first you credit the Almighty, and then you credit Nissen.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'm always sure if we wouldn't have Nissen we would be very bad. I don't know, maybe would survive, but.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever give up hope?

speaker: interviewee
text: It never came to me that I'm not going to survive. I knew that I'm going to survive. But I don't know. I thought that this was going to go on for years. That never, that we'll never be liberated. I though that this was going to go, we're going to be in this cave, and then go into the other cave.

speaker: interviewer
text: How today does your experience in the war affect you as man, as a parent, as somebody who is a citizen of the free world?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the good thing with me was, when I survived, I came out not, almost not touched, I would say. Only thing that I was touched was that a lot of family was missing, all my friends. But I had my mother and my father. I had my older sisters and brothers, you know. I had my family. So I was not touched like some other people terrible were touched. But the way the world is today, it's a very nice world. I hope they don't spoil it. The people are not too smart. They don't know what they have here in any place, all over. They should go in the cave a little bit, so maybe they'll change their way of thinking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do yo think such a Holocaust can happen to the Jewish people once again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. There's a lot of bad people here. When I see, for instance, [INAUDIBLE], talks, or this guy talks, and the skinheads all this here, hey, why, what as such. They have such beautiful country here, such good things. Everybody, everybody get dressed nice. And everybody has hot water, water in the house. You know that I grew up without electricity, without water in the house. I had to go run water. Who had running water? [INAUDIBLE]. They have too good here, people have too good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Stermer, in closing, can you share with us why you decided to do this testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: This has to be told. Other people wouldn't believe. Soon, soon they say never this happened. I know that I didn't tell the story like I would like to tell, but like my younger granddaughter, you're going to meet her soon. She says [INAUDIBLE], your English is not so good, but I still love you very much, she says.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing this time with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This was, this was my duty. It was my duty to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Stermer, would you explain the picture before us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Standing in the left side in the, in this uniform of Polish cavalry my brother Nissen. Next is my sister Hannah. Standing there is my brother Sam. And then it's me after Sam. And standing there is my late brother-in-law Fischel Dodek. And then on the right side is my sister Yetta. Now, sitting next to is from the left side is my mother, then my father, [INAUDIBLE], sitting in the middle, and my late sister Anna, Anna Dodek. And standing is a little [INAUDIBLE], her daughter Sonia. I think she was here two and a half or three years old, two and a half, maybe three years. Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was 1936, by the end of 1936, when my brother was in the Polish cavalry [INAUDIBLE] he came home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our town, in Korolowka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you describe the two people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep, this is my grandmother and my grandfather from my mother's side. This is Chaya and [INAUDIBLE] Sternhuss. My grandmother's maiden name was [? Orwitz. ?] She comes from a very good and famous family. My grandfather was killed in July 1920. The Ukrainian bandits, [NON-ENGLISH], they passed by and they came in to his house. They took him out and they just shot him in the front of his wife and his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken? Would you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken of course when a few years before, because they sent this picture, they sent this picture to the states. They had a daughter in South Bend, Indiana, a younger, a daughter. So they send this picture. And then I got it there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Could you please explain who is in the picture before us?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me, Saul Stermer, and my wife, Czarna on the honeymoon in 1946 and month of June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the P camp, in Austria, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Stermer, could you please explain this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding from one of our members from our cave [INAUDIBLE]. He lives in New York. And this is my brother Nissen on the right side. I'm in the left side. This is the most of the people from the cave, that we survive together. Not these children, this is already children, they were born after the war. My sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are people with whom you were in the cave?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the cave. In the cave, that they survived together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture is taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken I think in '52 or '53 near Montreal. They got married here. Today he lives in New York. He is the owner of [INAUDIBLE] hotels. He is very charitable. And he lost all his family. As a matter of fact, he had, when they killed his--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Everybody, a lot of people know him. He comes from a good family, his father and two brothers or three. In the ghetto in [INAUDIBLE] a place that we didn't like to go. They were shot in the house and he was shot too. And he was fell down, the blood from his brother was spilled on him. And he had like on the face, he had a little wound here. And he survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Stermer, would you explain about the manuscript in front of us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. She decided once that she has to put everything on paper so that one day the grandchildren and the great-grandchildren to read and to see what we went through through the years. So she went, she bought herself that book. And she was sitting and the writing and what happened to those years. And we are very grateful what she did. She did a wonderful thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: She wrote this, started to keep this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wrote this I think in 1960, [INAUDIBLE] when. She went with my sister to the country for the summer in [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE]. And she bought a book. She was sitting under the tree days and days. And she put everything together. Then we decided, this has to be translated. People have to read this what happened. And I think that was very important to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in fact, this was published as a book, is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was published as a book and the book is all over [INAUDIBLE]. And it's also in the, here in the Holocaust [INAUDIBLE] here and also in Washington. It's all over, the book here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did your mother pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother lived till 96. She passed away in '80, about 12 years ago, yeah, she passed away here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in the picture before us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my oldest grandson, Robbie, Reuben. It's his bar-mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is his second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lev, L-E-V. And he is right now, in the Yashiva in [INAUDIBLE]. He is very, very religious. I'm very happy about this here. He would like to be a Rabbi, but I don't think that he is going to be one. But you is a good kid. I love him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who is in the picture before us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my second grandson, Ari, Ari Lev.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is he?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where does he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Montreal. Today he is a counselor in the [INAUDIBLE] Camp. He's a very good boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Could you tell about this picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my two granddaughters from my son, Sydney. One is named Paige and Marissa. So wonderful girls

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are they today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 and 13. It was worth to fight. It was worth to fight and survive and to have grandchildren like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montreal, here in their house here. And the live here not far from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. And who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my youngest granddaughter, Aaron her name, Bonnie's daughter and Leo's.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine. She goes to [INAUDIBLE]. She is terrific student.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I understand that she invited you to come and speak about your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she invited me. Says, [INAUDIBLE], you have to tell this story from the cave to my friends. And I had to do it. And they were very smart kids. They asked me very smart questions. But she's telling me, [INAUDIBLE], your English is not so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, Mr. Stermer.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Stermer, you seem to be surrounded by many loving relatives here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give us the pleasure of introducing us to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: At my right is my brother, Nissen. Like I told before [INAUDIBLE], that my parents were blessed with Nissen. And we were lucky to have a brother Nissen. First the Almighty, then Nissen. In the left side is my sister Yetta. She was the best cook in the cave. But today, she doesn't want to cook stuff like that. And here is--

speaker: interviewee
text: The baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is the baby from the cave, [INAUDIBLE]. She was, how many years you were? Four years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was four years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years old. She was, she a lot time, I had a lot of fun there from her. I don't want to say now. And this in the back is my brother Sam. He was always watching we when we coming or going, he used to go too, with a small bag. Not a bag like Nissen's, but I used to carry a bag go with--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] bag.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] bag.

speaker: interviewee
text: He made me another one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on and Sam's left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's his wife, Bella. She survived--

speaker: interviewee
text: The forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the forest like. I'm not going to talk about this here. And in the back here is my wife, Czarna, a survivor. She survived with her sister, her mother, and brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your wife's maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Kimmerman. ?] Her father was killed on first day of [INAUDIBLE] in 1943 in Borschiv. As he was running, they killed him. And with his son he was running. The son survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 12th of April, 1946. And here is the flower girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to your wife, on her right?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one here is Aaron.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our youngest granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter. She is, it was, it was worth the fight to survive, with the family that we have [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to Aaron?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next to Aaron is, I'm Mrs. Stermer. I'm Nissen's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your first name is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Myrtle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Metzger. ?] My daughter, Brenda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Brenda, in the background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. We have two sons, five grandchildren. Unfortunately, they couldn't be here with us to share this occasion. But one day, I hope we will come together and we will do, redo some more work, what I went through personally.

speaker: interviewer
text: I look forward to it. And on your left side, what is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Peggy. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Yetta's daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Fay. And I have a brother also who is in Edmonton. And I have two children, a son of 22 and a daughter of 16. And unfortunately, they're not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father's family was all murdered in the Holocaust. And he died six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Abraham Katz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Brenda, I don't think we got-- BRENDA (NIECE): Yeah. We did? In the back on the right row, starting with the gentleman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Syd Stermer. Saul is my father. And this is my wife, Shelly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you do? And next to Shelly?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Lucy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lucy Stermer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my older.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] sister of and my brother and my sister here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm the oldest, and that's my dad and my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, and next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Bonnie, Saul's daughter, my husband Leo and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Our daughter Aaron.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I also have two kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took pictures of them. Yes,

speaker: interviewer
text: We got them in photographs.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Yeshiva and my other boy is in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I'd like to ask you in turn, first Nissen, because you played such a large role in your brother's testimony, how you feel about your brother giving this testimony and what you think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very happy that he's doing it. Because if he wouldn't do it, I will do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Stermer, is there anything that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say? I'm very happy that he did it. And it was a very important thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: May I ask the second generation, the children, if you have anything to say? Brenda? BRENDA (NIECE): Yeah, I was always very impressed by my father and the family. And I think that if there's anything that is, that I learned and that I respect the most is that this was a family that stood together. This was a family who went through a lot in life, and that they always knew in terms of a family, to be tight and to be together as a family. And that the brothers always sacrificed in terms of what was good for the family. And that's what made them strong. It was the ability to stand together as a family. And this quality seems to have come down to this generation, as well. BRENDA (NIECE): For the most part, yeah. And may I ask you, Mr. Stermer, up there on the right, Syd is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um hum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything that you would like to say, having been a child of survivors, how it impacted your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, growing up and hearing the story, hearing the stories over the years and it affected us greatly. And I'm very happy that he's able to come out and be able to tell other people about their experiences and what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], you should say something.
